content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 10, 2010


Marina Porche
Assistant County Counsel
800 South Victoria Avenue, L#1830
Ventura, California 93009

RE: Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-10-080

Dear Ms. Porche:

     This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift reporting provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

What is the value of the gift you received in attending a 501 (c)(3) nonprofit fundraising event where your admission was provided by your friend?

CONCLUSION

     The value is the face value if the admission privilege you received. The admission for your husband was a gift to him. Because the value of the admission privilege is more than the gift limit, you will need to pay down the amount to the $420 gift limit.

FACTS

     Recently, a business colleague and friend who sits on the board of a non-profit 501(c)(3) corporation invited you and your husband to attend a fundraising event for the organization, which included a musical performance, cocktails and dinner. When she invited you, she informed you that she was sponsoring a table at the event and that she would like to invite you and your husband to attend. While at the event on April 27, 2010, you first learned that the

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No.A-10-080
Page No. 2

tickets to the event cost $1,000 per couple. You have confirmed with your friend that the table
cost paid by your friend was $1,000 per couple for each of the 5 couples at your table. You have
also confirmed with your friend that the nondeductible tax portion per person attending was
$150, and the remaining $350 per person was a charitable contribution.

ANALYSIS

The Act defines gift as "any payment that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to
the extent the consideration of equal or greater value is not received . . . ." (Section 82028.)
Section 87207(a)(1) requires all public officials who are subject to the Act's disclosure
provisions² to report the name and address of the source of any reportable gift valued at $50 or
more. Additionally, the Act prohibits statutory and code filers from accepting gifts valued at
more than $420 from any one source in a calendar year if the employee would be required to
report the receipt of income or gifts from that source on his or her statement of economic
interests. (Section 89503(c); Regulation 18940.2.) You have received a gift from your friend³
and, as a category one filer required to report gifts from all sources, you are subject to the above
provisions.

Regulation 18946.1 provides that the value of an admission ticket is the face value of the
ticket "provided that the face value is the price that was . . . offered to the general public."
Admission to the event was offered to the general public through the organization's website (and
possibly other outlets).⁴ The price was $500 per person or $5,000 for a table of ten. This
comports with the information provided by your friend. The value of your admission is $500.
Because the admission was provided to both you and your husband, only the $500 value of your
ticket is attributable to you.⁵ Accordingly, you have received a gift of $500, which exceeds the
maximum allowable gift amount under the Act.

Regulation 18943(a) provides that any excess amount over the gift amount may be paid
down to avoid a violation of the Act provided that:

"[…]

² A public official is subject to the Act's disclosure provisions if he or she holds an office listed in Section
87200 (statutory filer) or is on her position is designated in the employing agency's conflict-of-interest code (code
filer). You have indicated that your position is designated in your conflict-of-interest code and that you have full
disclosure.

³ Although your friend sits on the board of a nonprofit, in our telephone conversation you indicated that the
admission privilege you received was paid for by your friend and given to you by her and not the nonprofit. If the
admission were provided directly from the nonprofit organization, Regulation 18946.4 would apply, and the gift
would have no value.

⁴ www.cameratapacifica.org/press/events/gala_sb.htm

⁵ Regulation 1894 defines when a gift made to a family member of an official is effectively a gift to the
official. Under this regulation, a ticket offered directly to the spouse of a local official is generally not considered to
be a gift to the official unless the person offering the gift is appearing before the local official, which is not the case
under your facts.

File No.A-10-080
Page No. 3

"(4) The recipient, within 30 days of receipt or acceptance, reimburses the
donor, or the donor's agent or intermediary, for all or a portion of the gift. In such
event the value of the gift is reduced by the amount of the reimbursement, and the
amount of any gift or activity expense which must be disclosed is reduced by the
amount of the reimbursement."

Accordingly, in order to be in compliance with the Act's gift limitation provision, you
must reimburse your friend $80 by May 27, 2010.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

By: William Lenkeit
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

WJL:jgl
